story: pivotItems — null items is treated as empty
scenario: getScores with null items (no score_sheet_items joined) returns empty scores, total 0
whyItMatters: Supabase may return null for empty join arrays; pivotItems must not crash.
risk: Missing || [] guard → TypeError crashing the entire score grid load.

story: pivotItems — null score_value kept as null, not added to total
scenario: Item with null score_value sets key to null in scores dict without adding to total
whyItMatters: Unanswered criteria must be distinguishable from a score of 0.
risk: Counting null as 0 inflates totals and misrepresents partial submissions.

story: pivotItems — item with no key is skipped
scenario: Item missing both period_criteria.key and criterion_key is silently skipped
whyItMatters: Corrupted or mis-mapped items must not crash or pollute the scores dict.
risk: undefined key in scores dict causes undefined property lookups in grid cells.

story: pivotItems — criterion_key fallback when period_criteria absent
scenario: Item without period_criteria join falls back to item.criterion_key for the dict key
whyItMatters: Legacy score rows may not have a period_criteria join; fallback preserves backward compat.
risk: Missing fallback → all legacy scores disappear from the grid.

story: pivotItems — period_criteria.key takes priority over criterion_key
scenario: When both period_criteria.key and criterion_key are present, period_criteria.key wins
whyItMatters: period_criteria.key is the authoritative per-period criterion identifier.
risk: Wrong key used → criterion columns misaligned in the score grid.

story: pivotItems — negative score values included in total
scenario: Item with negative score_value is recorded and subtracted from total
whyItMatters: Some rubric designs allow negative penalties; the pivot must not discard them.
risk: Dropping negatives silently inflates totals.

story: pivotItems — score value of 0 is included (not treated as falsy-skip)
scenario: Item with score_value 0 stores 0 in scores dict and adds 0 to total without crashing
whyItMatters: Zero is a valid rubric score; skipping it misrepresents unanswered vs. explicitly zero.
risk: Treating 0 as null would hide intentional zero scores in rankings.

story: pivotItems — string score_value is coerced to number
scenario: Item with score_value '85' (string) is stored as number 85
whyItMatters: Supabase numeric columns may arrive as strings in some query shapes.
risk: String concatenation instead of addition produces wrong totals (e.g. '8585' instead of 170).

story: pivotItems — multiple criteria in one sheet all appear in output
scenario: Sheet with three criteria items produces all three keys in scores dict
whyItMatters: All configured criteria must appear in the flattened row for grid rendering.
risk: Missing criterion key → grid cell blank or NaN for that criterion.

story: pivotItems — total is sum of all non-null numeric values
scenario: Sheet with values 70, null, 85 produces total 155 (null not counted)
whyItMatters: Total score drives rankings; partial answers must not inflate or deflate total.
risk: Counting null as 0 deflates total; skipping non-null corrupts ranking order.

story: getScores — DB error propagates as thrown error
scenario: When Supabase returns an error object, getScores throws rather than returning null data
whyItMatters: Callers rely on thrown errors to show error state; silent null would look like empty data.
risk: Swallowing DB errors → admin sees empty score grid with no indication of failure.

story: getScores — empty result set returns empty array
scenario: When Supabase returns empty data array, getScores returns []
whyItMatters: Periods with no submissions must show empty state, not crash.
risk: Crash or null return → score tab broken for new periods.

story: getScores — maps juror name and project title to output row
scenario: Output row contains juryName from juror join and projectName from project join
whyItMatters: Score grid displays juror and project names, not raw IDs.
risk: Missing name fields → entire row labels blank in the admin score table.

story: getProjectSummary — single juror total becomes project totalAvg
scenario: Project with one submitted sheet: totalAvg equals that sheet's pivot total
whyItMatters: With a single juror, avg and individual score must be identical.
risk: Division error or off-by-one → project rank shifts vs. expected.

scenario: Project with two submitted sheets: totalAvg = mean of both sheet totals
whyItMatters: Multi-juror averaging is the core ranking computation.
risk: Wrong denominator → ranking order wrong, academic fairness violated.

story: getProjectSummary — no submitted sheets → totalAvg null
scenario: Project with no submitted score sheets returns count=0 and totalAvg=null
whyItMatters: Unscored projects must show '-' in rankings, not 0.
risk: Returning 0 average for unscored projects misrepresents ranking status.

story: getProjectSummary — per-criterion avg computed correctly across jurors
scenario: Two jurors score criterion c1 as 80 and 60; avg.c1 = 70
whyItMatters: Criterion averages drive the analytics heatmap and accreditation charts.
risk: Wrong per-criterion avg → MÜDEK attainment rates wrong in exported reports.

scenario: Two projects with different juror sets each receive their own correct avg
whyItMatters: Projects must not bleed score data into each other.
risk: Aggregation bug → projects share averaged scores, entire ranking wrong.

story: getScores — console.warn emitted when result length equals SCORE_QUERY_CAP
scenario: When Supabase returns exactly SCORE_QUERY_CAP rows, a console.warn is emitted
whyItMatters: Operators need visibility when the row cap is hit to know aggregation may be incomplete.
risk: Silent cap → admin sees truncated scores with no indication that data is missing.

story: getProjectSummary — DB error throws rather than returning partial data
scenario: When the score_sheets query returns an error, getProjectSummary throws
whyItMatters: Partial data (projects loaded but sheets failed) must surface as an error, not a misleading empty ranking.
risk: Swallowing sheet error → rankings show all projects with null avg, looks like a valid unscored period.

story: getScores — RLS permission denied (42501) propagates as thrown error
scenario: When Supabase returns error code 42501 (permission denied), getScores throws the error
whyItMatters: RLS is the primary cross-org data barrier; if errors are swallowed, a blocked query looks like empty data.
risk: Silent RLS failure → attacker sees empty state, retries with different period IDs; no alert to ops.

story: getProjectSummary — RLS permission denied propagates as thrown error
scenario: When the projects query returns error code 42501, getProjectSummary throws
whyItMatters: Project summary is the main ranking data source; a silenced RLS block would show an empty ranking that looks valid.
risk: Swallowed 42501 → ranking tab shows empty with no error, admins assume no data rather than a security block.

story: getScores — undefined periodId passed to query without crash
scenario: Calling getScores(undefined) does not throw a client-side error before reaching Supabase
whyItMatters: Component bugs may pass undefined periodId; the function must not crash before the DB can respond.
risk: Client crash → unhandled error surfaces as blank page instead of a recoverable error state.

story: getScores — null periodId propagates to query layer, not client crash
scenario: Calling getScores(null) passes null to the supabase .eq() call without throwing
whyItMatters: null periodId must be caught at the DB (RLS/constraint), not silently crash the client.
risk: Client-side crash → unhandled promise rejection, score grid permanently broken.

story: pivotItems — no org_id filtering: cross-org data leakage gap documented
scenario: When getScores returns rows containing two different org_id values, both are included in output
whyItMatters: Documents that pivotItems has no client-side org filter; RLS is the sole boundary.
risk: If RLS fails, getScores returns cross-org data with no client-side safety net.

story: listJurorsSummary — RLS permission denied propagates as thrown error
scenario: When any of the three parallel queries in listJurorsSummary returns 42501, the function throws
whyItMatters: Juror summary lists all jurors for a period; a silenced RLS block would show empty juror list as valid.
risk: Swallowed error → admin sees no jurors, assumes none assigned rather than security block.

story: writeGroup session expired (P0401) sets sessionExpired=true and editLockActive=true
scenario: When upsertScore throws P0401, writeGroup sets sessionExpired=true, editLockActive=true, and returns false
whyItMatters: Session expiry must block all further writes and show a targeted 'session expired' message — not a generic error.
risk: If sessionExpired is not set, the UI shows a generic error and jurors retry infinitely without knowing their session is gone.

story: writeGroup period lock sets editLockActive=true without setting sessionExpired
scenario: When upsertScore throws with message 'period_locked', writeGroup sets editLockActive=true but not sessionExpired
whyItMatters: Period lock and session expiry have different UX: period lock shows 'evaluation ended' not 'session expired'.
risk: Confusing UX: juror told session expired when the problem is the period was closed.

story: writeGroup treats final_submit_required as a successful skip (returns true)
scenario: When upsertScore throws 'final_submit_required', writeGroup returns true instead of false
whyItMatters: Juror already submitted — score is persisted. Treating this as an error would show a false save failure.
risk: False error banner on submitted jurors; they may attempt re-submission unnecessarily.

story: writeGroup network/generic error sets saveStatus=error and returns false
scenario: When upsertScore throws a plain Error (no special code), writeGroup sets saveStatus=error and returns false
whyItMatters: Network failures during autosave must surface to the user so they know their work is not saved.
risk: Silent data loss — juror completes evaluation but scores never reach the DB.

story: writeGroup returns false immediately when editLockActive is true
scenario: When editLockActive=true, writeGroup does not call upsertScore and returns false
whyItMatters: Edit lock prevents writes when the period is closed or session has expired — bypassing it would create phantom writes.
risk: Score writes after period close could corrupt audit trail or create score/sheet mismatches.

story: writeGroup returns false when required fields (jurorId, sessionToken, periodId) are missing
scenario: When stateRef.current has no sessionToken, writeGroup returns false without calling upsertScore
whyItMatters: Prevents upsertScore from being called with null auth data, which would result in an RLS error.
risk: Null RPC call → DB error or phantom write with null juror_id.

story: writeGroup deduplication skips second identical write (lastWrittenRef)
scenario: When writeGroup is called twice with identical snapshot data, upsertScore is called only once
whyItMatters: onChange fires on every keystroke; without deduplication every blur/visibility event would trigger a DB write.
risk: Excessive RPC calls → DB rate limit, juror UI lag, wasted network bandwidth.

story: writeGroup concurrent same-pid write is skipped (inFlightRef guard)
scenario: When a writeGroup for pid-1 is in progress and another writeGroup for the same pid arrives, the second call returns true without calling upsertScore again
whyItMatters: Concurrent writes for the same project could cause race conditions or conflicting score rows.
risk: Race condition: two concurrent upserts for same (period, project, juror) triple → DB conflict error.

story: visibilitychange to hidden triggers writeGroup when step=eval
scenario: When document fires visibilitychange to 'hidden' and step is 'eval', writeGroup is called with the current project id
whyItMatters: Mobile jurors frequently switch apps — autosave on background prevents score loss.
risk: Mobile juror backgrounded app → score not saved → data loss on session expiry.

story: visibilitychange to hidden does NOT trigger writeGroup when step is not eval
scenario: When document fires visibilitychange to 'hidden' and step is 'pin', writeGroup is not called
whyItMatters: Non-eval steps have no meaningful score state to save — spurious saves would fail or create empty score rows.
risk: Spurious save in non-eval step → RPC called with undefined project ID → DB error.

story: Background visibility save failure is re-surfaced when tab becomes visible
scenario: When writeGroup fails while hidden and the user returns (visible), saveStatus becomes 'error'
whyItMatters: Errors during background saves are invisible — the user only returns to the tab AFTER the error occurs.
risk: Silent data loss: juror thinks scores are saved but they never were because the background save failed.

area: useJuryAutosave — session expired sets both flags
story: P0401 error sets both sessionExpired=true and editLockActive=true atomically
scenario: After a P0401 throw from upsertScore, both sessionExpired and editLockActive are true
whyItMatters: Both must be set together: editLockActive prevents further writes while sessionExpired drives the UX branch.
risk: If only editLockActive is set, the juror sees a generic lock screen without a targeted 'session expired' message.

story: getActiveEntryToken: no token row → returns false
scenario: When entry_tokens has no row for the period, getActiveEntryToken returns false
whyItMatters: Admin must know there's no active token before generating one.
risk: Stale UI showing 'token active' when none exists.

story: getActiveEntryToken: token without expires_at → returns true (no expiry = always valid)
scenario: When entry_tokens row has expires_at=null, getActiveEntryToken returns true
whyItMatters: Non-expiring tokens must be treated as active, not as invalid.
risk: Perpetual tokens incorrectly shown as expired → juror entry blocked.

story: getActiveEntryToken: token with future expires_at → returns true
scenario: When expires_at is 24h in the future, getActiveEntryToken returns true
whyItMatters: Active tokens must allow juror entry.
risk: Active token treated as expired → jurors cannot enter.

story: getActiveEntryToken: token with past expires_at → returns false
scenario: When expires_at is 1h in the past, getActiveEntryToken returns false
whyItMatters: Expired tokens must not grant entry.
risk: Expired token treated as active → unauthorized juror entry after evaluation period ends.

story: getActiveEntryToken: malformed expires_at string → treated as no expiry (returns true)
scenario: When expires_at is 'not-a-date', Date.parse returns NaN and getActiveEntryToken returns true
whyItMatters: DB data corruption (bad expires_at) must not silently block juror entry.
risk: Corrupt date string causes false 'expired' result → entry blocked with no visible error.

scenario: When supabase returns an error, getActiveEntryToken throws the error
whyItMatters: DB errors must be surfaced so callers can show an error state instead of silently returning false.
risk: Swallowed DB error → admin sees 'no token' when the real problem is a permission or connectivity error.

story: getActiveEntryTokenPlain: active token → returns token_plain string
scenario: When a non-revoked token with null expires_at exists, getActiveEntryTokenPlain returns the token_plain value
whyItMatters: Admin copy-pastes the plain token to share with jurors — this is the access path.
risk: Returning null instead of the plain token → admin cannot retrieve the entry token.

scenario: When token has past expires_at, getActiveEntryTokenPlain returns null
whyItMatters: Expired token plain value must not be returned — it cannot be used for juror entry.
risk: Expired token shown to admin as active → confusion when jurors cannot enter.

story: getActiveEntryTokenPlain: no token row → returns null
scenario: When entry_tokens has no row, getActiveEntryTokenPlain returns null
whyItMatters: Missing token must return null, not throw an error.
risk: Uncaught exception instead of graceful null → admin panel error state.

story: getEntryTokenHistory: no tokens → returns empty array
scenario: When entry_tokens has no rows for the period, getEntryTokenHistory returns []
whyItMatters: Empty history is valid; returning null would crash the history table component.
risk: Uncaught null access on .map() in the history table component.

story: getEntryTokenHistory: active token — session_count equals raw update count
scenario: For a non-revoked token with 3 update events, session_count is 3
whyItMatters: Non-revoked tokens must show the full session count without deduction.
risk: Understating session count: admin cannot accurately assess how many jurors entered.

story: getEntryTokenHistory: revoked token — session_count is rawCount minus 1 (revoke event excluded)
scenario: For a revoked token with 3 update events (including the revoke), session_count is 2
whyItMatters: The revoke operation creates one update event that must not be counted as a juror session.
risk: Over-counting: revoke event counted as session → inflated entry counts.

story: getEntryTokenHistory: revoked token with 0 updates — session_count is 0, not -1
scenario: For a revoked token with 0 audit update events, session_count is clamped to 0
whyItMatters: A revoke with no prior sessions must show 0, not -1 (negative session count makes no sense).
risk: Negative session count renders as '-1' in the admin table → confusing UX.

story: getEntryTokenHistory: access_id is derived from token_plain and formatted XXXX-XXXX
scenario: For a token with token_plain='abcd1234xyz', access_id is the first 8 alphanumeric chars uppercased in XXXX-XXXX format
whyItMatters: Admin uses the access_id to identify tokens in support scenarios — it must match what was communicated.
risk: Wrong access_id shown → admin cannot match a reported token to the DB record.

story: getEntryTokenHistory: active token has correct is_active/is_revoked/is_expired flags
scenario: Active token (not revoked, future expiry) has is_active=true, is_revoked=false, is_expired=false
whyItMatters: UI renders different badges based on these flags — incorrect flags show wrong badge.
risk: Expired token shown as active → admin does not know to generate a new token.

story: setEvalLock(periodId, true) calls rpc_admin_set_period_lock with p_locked=true
scenario: setEvalLock('p-1', true) resolves without throwing and calls the RPC with p_locked: true
whyItMatters: Locking is the primary protection mechanism — if the RPC is called with wrong p_locked value, the period is incorrectly unlocked.
risk: Wrong boolean coercion → period unlocked instead of locked during evaluation.

story: setEvalLock(periodId, false) calls rpc_admin_set_period_lock with p_locked=false
scenario: setEvalLock('p-1', false) calls the RPC with p_locked: false
whyItMatters: Unlock must send false not true — a coercion bug would lock instead of unlock.
risk: Admin tries to unlock period but it stays locked → jurors cannot start scoring.

story: setEvalLock coerces enabled to boolean via !! (truthy string → true)
scenario: setEvalLock('p-1', 'yes') passes p_locked: true (boolean) to the RPC
whyItMatters: The RPC parameter must be a boolean — string 'yes' would fail the DB parameter type check.
risk: DB type error → lock call silently fails → period in wrong state.

scenario: When rpc returns error, setEvalLock throws the error
whyItMatters: Lock failures must surface to the admin UI — silently swallowing them would leave the period in an unknown lock state.
risk: Admin sees success flash but period was not locked → evaluation proceeds on an unlocked period.

story: publishPeriod: success → returns data from RPC
scenario: publishPeriod('p-1') resolves with the RPC return value
whyItMatters: publishPeriod is the gated transition to live status — must return data for UI to update.
risk: Silent return of undefined → UI does not update period status to 'published'.

story: publishPeriod: already published → returns { ok: true, already_published: true }
scenario: publishPeriod is idempotent — calling it twice does not throw
whyItMatters: Network retries or double-clicks must not cause errors on re-publish.
risk: Second publish call throws → admin sees error flash on innocent retry.

scenario: Calling publishPeriod(null) throws with a descriptive message before reaching the DB
whyItMatters: Client-side guard prevents a null RPC call that could fail silently or return unexpected data.
risk: Null RPC call → ambiguous DB response or accidental publish of wrong period.

scenario: closePeriod('p-1') resolves with RPC return data
whyItMatters: Period closure is a terminal state transition — must confirm success.
risk: UI does not update closed_at → period appears still open.

scenario: Calling closePeriod(null) throws with a descriptive message
whyItMatters: Null period close would be an unrecoverable DB error.
risk: Null RPC call → DB error or accidental closure of wrong period.

story: setEvalLock called twice with same value does not throw
scenario: Calling setEvalLock('p-1', true) twice resolves successfully both times (idempotent)
whyItMatters: Network retries or concurrent admin actions must not cause errors.
risk: Second lock call throws → admin UI shows error after a network retry of a succeeded request.

scenario: fetch returns 200 with JSON body — invokeEdgeFunction returns { data: <parsed body>, error: null }
whyItMatters: This is the happy path — all edge function callers depend on this contract.
risk: Incorrect return shape breaks every feature that calls an edge function.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: first fetch 401 → refresh → retry → 200 succeeds
scenario: First fetch returns 401, refreshSession returns new token, second fetch returns 200 with data
whyItMatters: Stale JWT is the most common auth failure — transparent refresh prevents session interruptions.
risk: Without retry, every tab-return triggers auth errors for all edge function callers.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: first 401 → refresh → retry still 401 → returns Session expired error
scenario: Both fetches return 401 — invokeEdgeFunction returns { data: null, error: Error('Session expired...') }
whyItMatters: After refresh fails, callers need a sentinel error to show the user a 'please reload' message.
risk: Caller receives null data with no error → silent failure, no UX feedback.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: 403 response → returns error with response text
scenario: fetch returns 403 — invokeEdgeFunction returns { data: null, error: Error(<response text>) }
whyItMatters: Permission errors from the function must reach the caller to show a meaningful error message.
risk: 403 swallowed → caller sees no error, shows partial/stale data instead of 'not allowed' message.

scenario: fetch returns 500 — invokeEdgeFunction returns { data: null, error: Error(<text>) }
whyItMatters: Server errors must surface to callers for error UI.
risk: Silent 500 → feature appears broken with no error shown to user.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: fetch throws TypeError → exception propagates (not caught)
scenario: When fetch itself throws (network unavailable), invokeEdgeFunction does not catch it — the error propagates to the caller
whyItMatters: Documents the architectural contract: network errors throw rather than return error object — callers must use try-catch.
risk: Callers expecting { error } return will not catch a network failure, causing an unhandled rejection.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: non-JSON 200 response → returns Invalid JSON error object
scenario: fetch returns 200 but body is not valid JSON — invokeEdgeFunction returns { data: null, error: Error('Invalid JSON response...') }
whyItMatters: Some edge functions may return HTML error pages on misconfiguration — must not throw.
risk: JSON parse error throws instead of returning error object → unhandled rejection in caller.

story: invokeEdgeFunction serializes body as JSON string in POST request
scenario: When body is { key: 'val' }, fetch is called with body: '{"key":"val"}'
whyItMatters: Edge functions receive raw POST body — if it is not JSON-stringified the function receives '[object Object]'.
risk: Edge function receives garbled body → silent data corruption with no error returned.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: called with no body → fetch body is undefined (not 'undefined' string)
scenario: When invoked without body option, requestBody is undefined and fetch is called with body: undefined
whyItMatters: Sending 'undefined' string as body would break functions that validate the body.
risk: Stringified 'undefined' body causes JSON parse errors in the edge function.

story: Kong 401 and function-internal 401 both trigger the same refresh+retry path (architectural note)
scenario: A 401 from Kong (execution_time_ms ≈ 0) and from the function itself both cause one refresh+retry — no distinction is made
whyItMatters: Documents the architectural gap: Kong pre-rejection and function rejection behave identically from invokeEdgeFunction's perspective.
risk: Function-internal 401 triggers a useless refresh — but there is no adverse effect beyond one extra network call.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: session expiring within 30s → proactive refresh in getValidSession
scenario: getSession returns a session with expires_at within 30 seconds — invokeEdgeFunction calls refreshSession before the first fetch
whyItMatters: Stale JWT causes immediate 401 on first call — pre-refresh avoids the retry round-trip.
risk: Without pre-refresh, every near-expiry session triggers a 401 + refresh + retry — double latency on edge calls.

story: invokeEdgeFunction: no session → fetch is made without Authorization header but with apikey
scenario: When getSession returns null, the fetch request omits the Authorization header but still includes apikey
whyItMatters: Some edge functions are callable without auth (anon key only) — missing the apikey would cause Kong rejection.
risk: Kong rejects the request as unauthenticated if apikey header is missing.

story: AbortError with steal-lock message → retry triggered (getSession called again)
scenario: When getSession throws AbortError('Lock broken by another request with the steal option'), AuthProvider retries getSession
whyItMatters: Lock contention during startup causes legitimate AbortErrors that should be retried, not crash the app.
risk: No retry → AuthProvider fails to boot, loading state hangs, user cannot log in.

story: AbortError with 'was not released within' message → retry triggered
scenario: When getSession throws AbortError('was not released within 5000ms'), AuthProvider retries
whyItMatters: Both lock error variants must trigger retry — missing the second variant leaves one common boot failure unrecovered.
risk: Lock timeout error type causes auth hang on affected browsers/iOS.

story: Non-AbortError → not recoverable (getSession called only once, error propagates)
scenario: When getSession throws a plain TypeError('network'), AuthProvider does NOT retry — getSession called exactly once
whyItMatters: Non-lock errors (network, parse) must not be silently retried — they would hide real failures.
risk: Infinite retry loop on non-recoverable errors → hang at loading screen.

story: AbortError with unrelated message → not recoverable (no retry)
scenario: When getSession throws AbortError('user aborted'), AuthProvider does NOT retry
whyItMatters: Not all AbortErrors are lock-related — user-triggered aborts must not be retried.
risk: Unrelated AbortError silently retried 3 times → 360ms extra delay on page load.

story: null/undefined error → isRecoverableAuthLockError returns false without throwing
scenario: getSessionWithRetry catches null — isRecoverableAuthLockError(null) returns false, error rethrown
whyItMatters: Defensive handling: if error is null/undefined, the guard must not throw itself.
risk: TypeError in catch block → unhandled exception during auth bootstrap.

story: getSessionWithRetry: first attempt succeeds → getSession called exactly once
scenario: When getSession succeeds on first attempt, no retry happens
whyItMatters: Happy path: no unnecessary extra calls to getSession.
risk: Extra getSession calls on every load → unnecessary latency and token refresh.

story: getSessionWithRetry: first attempt throws recoverable error, second succeeds → 2 total getSession calls
scenario: getSession throws AbortError(steal) on attempt 1, succeeds on attempt 2 — getSession called exactly twice
whyItMatters: Single lock contention is the common transient case — must recover on the second attempt.
risk: No recovery → auth fails on the most common transient case.

story: getSessionWithRetry: 3 consecutive recoverable errors → throws after max attempts
scenario: getSession throws AbortError(steal) 3 times — after 3 attempts, the error propagates (getSession called 3 times)
whyItMatters: Lock contention that persists for 3 attempts is a real problem that should not be silently ignored.
risk: Infinite retry if max-attempts guard is wrong → loading screen hangs indefinitely.

story: getSessionWithRetry: non-recoverable error on first attempt → throws immediately (1 call only)
scenario: When getSession throws TypeError('network failed'), it throws after 1 attempt (not 3)
whyItMatters: Non-lock errors must not waste 2 extra attempts with waits before failing.
risk: Network errors cause 360ms extra delay during auth bootstrap before failing.

story: getSessionWithRetry: backoff is 120ms on first retry, 240ms on second
scenario: Retry waits follow 120 * (i+1) pattern using fake timers
whyItMatters: Too-short backoff races the lock; too-long causes visible loading delays.
risk: Wrong backoff → still hitting the lock on retry or 500ms+ auth delay on mobile.

story: getSessionWithRetry: near-expiry session → proactively calls refreshSession
scenario: getSession returns session with expires_at within 30 seconds — refreshSession is called before returning
whyItMatters: Avoids the bootstrap flow running with a token that expires within seconds.
risk: Near-expiry JWT used for membership fetch → Realtime subscription fails immediately.

area: AuthProvider — catch block after exhausted retries
story: AuthProvider: after getSessionWithRetry exhausts retries, loading=false (not stuck)
scenario: When getSessionWithRetry throws (after 3 lock errors), AuthProvider sets loading=false so the user sees the login screen
whyItMatters: Even after retry exhaustion, the app must reach an idle state so the user can log in.
risk: AuthProvider stuck in loading=true → blank loading spinner with no way to proceed.

story: fullExport: returns all data sections when DB queries succeed
scenario: fullExport('org-1') returns { periods, projects, jurors, scores, audit_logs } when all queries succeed
whyItMatters: The admin export must contain complete data — missing a section silently creates an incomplete export file.
risk: Missing scores/periods in export → admin submits incomplete CSV to accreditation body.

scenario: When periods query returns error, fullExport throws the error
whyItMatters: Export must fail loudly — a partial export (missing periods) is worse than no export.
risk: Export proceeds with empty periods → scores reference non-existent period IDs in output.

scenario: When jurors query returns error, fullExport throws
whyItMatters: Missing juror data in export means score sheets cannot be correlated to jurors.
risk: Partial export with scores but no jurors → unusable accreditation data.

story: fullExport: org with no periods → returns empty datasets without throwing
scenario: When org has no periods, fullExport returns { periods: [], projects: [], scores: [], jurors: [...], audit_logs: [...] }
whyItMatters: Empty org export must not throw — new orgs with no data must return an empty export.
risk: Null access on empty periodIds array → crash when generating an export for a new org.

scenario: logExportInitiated({ action: 'export.scores', organizationId: 'org-1' }) resolves when edge function succeeds
whyItMatters: The audit write must succeed before the file is generated — callers depend on a clean resolve.
risk: Audit log silent fail → export proceeds without a server-side record — regulatory compliance gap.

story: logExportInitiated: edge function error → throws (caller must abort export)
scenario: When invokeEdgeFunction returns { error: new Error('...') }, logExportInitiated throws
whyItMatters: Blocking audit write — if the write fails, the export must be aborted.
risk: Export proceeds without audit log → compliance violation during external audit.

story: logExportInitiated: action not starting with 'export.' → throws before calling edge function
scenario: logExportInitiated({ action: 'scores.download' }) throws 'action must start with export.' without calling invokeEdgeFunction
whyItMatters: Audit action namespace enforces that all exported events are categorized under 'export.*'.
risk: Miscategorized audit action → wrong event taxonomy, breaking audit log filters.

story: logExportInitiated: edge function returns data.error string → throws wrapped error
scenario: When invokeEdgeFunction returns { data: { error: 'unauthorized' }, error: null }, logExportInitiated throws Error containing 'unauthorized'
whyItMatters: Some edge functions return errors in the response body — must be propagated.
risk: Function-body error silently swallowed → export proceeds without an audit log record.

story: logExportInitiated throws → caller does not proceed to generate file (abort contract documented)
scenario: When logExportInitiated throws, the export sequence stops — no file generation follows
whyItMatters: The audit write is the export gate — failure must prevent the file from being generated.
risk: File generated without audit log → user gets file with no server-side record of the download.

story: fullExport: audit_logs query error is NOT checked — failure returns empty array (gap documented)
scenario: fullExport does not check auditRes.error — audit_logs failure returns empty array silently
whyItMatters: Documents that audit_logs query errors are not surfaced — gap if audit logs are required.
risk: Audit logs query fails silently → export file has empty audit section with no warning.

scenario: value written by setJuryAccess is returned by getJuryAccess
whyItMatters: Jury access state must survive across reads within the same session.
risk: Broken round-trip causes jury flow to restart or fail on each page load.

story: setJuryAccess uses KEYS constant, not hardcoded string
scenario: the localStorage key used matches KEYS.JURY_ACCESS exactly
whyItMatters: Hardcoded key strings cause namespace collisions and bypass the centralized key registry.
risk: Two modules writing different keys under the same string silently corrupt each other's state.

story: setJuryAccess does not throw when storage is full
scenario: QuotaExceededError from both localStorage and sessionStorage is swallowed
whyItMatters: Safari private mode and full storage throw on writes; uncaught errors crash the UI.
risk: App throws unhandled exception; jury flow breaks for all Safari private-mode users.

story: getJuryAccess / setJuryAccess survive SecurityError from both storages
scenario: SecurityError from both storages is caught; get returns null, set does not throw
whyItMatters: iOS Safari private mode disables all storage; uncaught errors crash every jury page.
risk: All Safari private-mode users see a crash instead of a graceful fallback.

story: getJuryAccessGrant returns null when stored value is corrupt JSON
scenario: malformed JSON in both storages → getJuryAccessGrant returns null without throwing
whyItMatters: Corrupt storage must not propagate a SyntaxError that crashes the app on load.
risk: Uncaught JSON parse error breaks jury entry for all users whose storage is corrupt.

story: JURY_ and ADMIN_ key prefixes are distinct
scenario: JURY_ACCESS prefix != ADMIN_UI_STATE prefix; no key collision between namespaces
whyItMatters: Key collisions between jury and admin state would corrupt one when the other writes.
risk: Admin state overwrites jury access or vice-versa, causing unexpected logouts or access errors.

story: setJuryAccessGrant dual-writes to both localStorage and sessionStorage
scenario: after setJuryAccessGrant, JURY_ACCESS_GRANT key appears in both storage setItem calls
whyItMatters: Dual-write ensures jury access grant survives both tab restarts and cross-tab navigation.
risk: Single-write means jury must re-authenticate after any tab refresh.

story: clearJuryAccess removes JURY_ACCESS from both storages
scenario: after clearJuryAccess, removeItem is called with JURY_ACCESS key on both ls and ss
whyItMatters: Partial clear leaves stale access in one storage, allowing unauthorized re-entry.
risk: User appears logged-out in one tab but can re-enter via the other storage.

story: saveJurySession writes all 6 session keys to localStorage
scenario: after saveJurySession, all 6 KEYS constants appear in ls.setItem call keys
whyItMatters: Missing keys cause getJurySession to return null, forcing juror to re-authenticate.
risk: Juror loses evaluation progress and must restart the jury flow from scratch.

story: getJurySession returns null when storage is empty
scenario: with nothing in storage, getJurySession returns null
whyItMatters: Null return triggers proper re-authentication instead of crashing on missing fields.
risk: Returning undefined or partial object causes downstream null-dereference crashes.

scenario: all 7 session fields written by saveJurySession are returned intact by getJurySession
whyItMatters: Complete session restoration prevents juror from losing their place in the evaluation flow.
risk: Missing field causes wrong juror identity or period, corrupting score submissions.

story: clearJurySession removes session token, juror id, and period id from localStorage
scenario: after clearJurySession, the 3 core session keys appear in ls.removeItem calls
whyItMatters: Incomplete clear leaves stale tokens; next user on the same device gets wrong identity.
risk: Stale session token used by next user exposes previous juror's scoring data.

story: getRawToken returns null for unknown period
scenario: getRawToken for a period with no stored token returns null
whyItMatters: Null return must not crash; caller decides to prompt for token instead.
risk: Returning undefined breaks strict null checks in token validation logic.

story: setRawToken dual-writes to both storages with JURY_RAW_TOKEN_PREFIX key
scenario: after setRawToken, the prefixed key appears in both ls.setItem and ss.setItem calls
whyItMatters: Raw tokens must survive tab refresh (ls) and be available to current tab (ss).
risk: Missing dual-write causes token loss on refresh, requiring admin to re-enter the token.

story: clearRawToken removes the prefixed key from both storages
scenario: after clearRawToken, the prefixed key appears in both ls.removeItem and ss.removeItem calls
whyItMatters: Partial removal leaves stale token in one storage; revoked tokens remain accessible.
risk: Revoked token persists in one storage, allowing re-entry after token revocation.

story: setRawToken does not throw on QuotaExceededError
scenario: QuotaExceededError from both storages is swallowed by setRawToken
whyItMatters: Full storage must not crash the admin token flow.
risk: Uncaught quota error crashes admin UI on devices with full storage.

story: JURY_RAW_TOKEN_PREFIX key does not collide with session or access keys
scenario: raw token key differs from JURY_SESSION_TOKEN, JURY_ACCESS, and ADMIN_UI_STATE
whyItMatters: Key collision would corrupt session or access state when a raw token is stored.
risk: Token write silently overwrites session token, logging juror out mid-evaluation.

story: getCriteriaScratch returns null for unknown period
scenario: getCriteriaScratch for period with no scratch returns null
whyItMatters: Null return triggers the empty-state UI path, not a crash.
risk: Returning undefined causes downstream JSON operations to throw.

scenario: after setCriteriaScratch, the key appears in ss.setItem but criteria scratch is readable
whyItMatters: Criteria scratch is ephemeral per-tab state; writing to localStorage would persist stale drafts across sessions.
risk: Old draft appears after browser restart, overwriting admin's latest criteria.

story: writeSection / readSection round-trip for admin UI state
scenario: data written by writeSection is returned intact by readSection for the same key
whyItMatters: Admin UI state persistence (active tab, sort, filters) must survive page reloads.
risk: Broken round-trip resets all admin UI preferences on every refresh.

story: readSection returns {} for missing key and {} for corrupt JSON
scenario: nonexistent section returns {}; corrupt JSON in storage returns {}
whyItMatters: Corrupt or missing storage must yield an empty object, not a crash.
risk: SyntaxError propagates up and crashes the admin panel on load.

story: writeSection does not throw on QuotaExceededError
scenario: QuotaExceededError from localStorage is swallowed by writeSection
whyItMatters: Full storage must not crash the admin panel when saving UI preferences.
risk: Unhandled quota error kills the admin UI for users on storage-full devices.

story: getActiveOrganizationId returns null when nothing is stored
scenario: getActiveOrganizationId with empty storage returns null
whyItMatters: Null return means no org filter is active; undefined would crash the tenant-scope check.
risk: Returning undefined causes super-admin org selector to malfunction.

story: setActiveOrganizationId stores value; null clears via removeItem
scenario: setActiveOrganizationId('org-1') is readable; setActiveOrganizationId(null) calls removeItem
whyItMatters: Org selection must be clearable without leaving a 'null' string in storage.
risk: String 'null' in storage causes org filter to treat null-string as a real org ID.

scenario: anomalyDedupKey('ip_multi_org', {ip_address:'1.2.3.4'}) returns 'ip_multi_org:1.2.3.4'
whyItMatters: Correct dedup key prevents the same IP anomaly from being written multiple times per sweep.
risk: Wrong key format causes all anomalies to be re-written every hour, flooding the audit log.

story: anomalyDedupKey falls back to organization_id when ip_address absent
scenario: anomalyDedupKey('pin_flood', {organization_id:'org-1'}) returns 'pin_flood:org-1'
whyItMatters: Org-bound anomaly types have no IP address; org_id is the correct discriminator.
risk: Missing fallback causes all org anomalies to collide under '__global__', masking real threats.

story: anomalyDedupKey returns __global__ when neither ip nor org present
scenario: anomalyDedupKey('org_suspended', {}) returns 'org_suspended:__global__'
whyItMatters: Global anomalies (chain broken, suspended org) must have a stable dedup key.
risk: Empty discriminator produces 'org_suspended:' which collides with nothing but is semantically wrong.

story: detailsDedupKey reconstructs the same key from a previously-written details blob
scenario: detailsDedupKey({anomaly_type:'ip_multi_org',ip_address:'1.2.3.4'}) matches anomalyDedupKey output
whyItMatters: Deduplication across sweeps requires reading back the key that was written in the previous sweep.
risk: Key mismatch causes every anomaly to be re-written on the next sweep, creating noise.

story: detailsDedupKey uses organization_id when ip_address absent in details blob
scenario: detailsDedupKey({anomaly_type:'pin_flood',organization_id:'org-2'}) returns 'pin_flood:org-2'
whyItMatters: Org-bound anomalies written in the previous sweep must be deduped correctly by org.
risk: Wrong discriminator skips dedup, causing the same pin_flood anomaly to be written every hour.

story: detailsDedupKey handles empty or missing fields gracefully
scenario: detailsDedupKey({}) returns ':__global__' without throwing
whyItMatters: Malformed rows in audit_logs must not crash the sweep — they should be treated as unknown.
risk: TypeError on missing field kills the entire sweep, leaving security anomalies undetected.

story: ip_multi_org fires when same IP appears across ≥2 distinct orgs
scenario: 2 audit rows with same IP but different org_ids satisfy the ip_multi_org threshold
whyItMatters: Cross-org IP activity is a critical security signal for credential sharing or attack.
risk: Missed detection allows credential stuffing or lateral movement to go unnoticed.

area: ip_multi_org rule — single org does not fire
story: ip_multi_org does NOT fire when same IP appears in only one org
scenario: 2 rows with same IP + same org_id do not satisfy ip_multi_org threshold
whyItMatters: False positives from single-org IPs create alert fatigue and mask real threats.
risk: Admin receives spurious anomaly alerts, begins ignoring them, misses real attack.

story: pin_flood fires at ≥10 juror.pin_locked events per org
scenario: 10 pin_locked rows for same org satisfies pin_flood; 9 does not
whyItMatters: PIN flood detection catches brute-force attacks on the jury access screen.
risk: Wrong threshold either misses real attacks (too high) or creates noise (too low).

story: export_burst fires at ≥5 export.* events per org
scenario: 5 rows matching 'export.*' action for same org satisfies export_burst; 4 does not
whyItMatters: Rapid bulk exports may indicate data exfiltration by a compromised admin account.
risk: Undetected export burst allows attacker to download all org data before revocation.

scenario: escapeHtml('a & b') returns 'a & b'
whyItMatters: Unescaped HTML in email bodies enables XSS if email clients render raw HTML.
risk: Juror name containing '&' renders broken HTML or enables script injection in email.

story: escapeHtml escapes all 5 HTML special characters
scenario: escapeHtml escapes &, <, >, ", ' in a single input string
whyItMatters: All 5 characters must be escaped to prevent XSS in HTML email templates.
risk: Any unescaped character allows script injection or malformed HTML in admin email.

scenario: escapeHtml('hello world') returns 'hello world'
whyItMatters: Over-escaping normal strings would corrupt juror names or affiliation text in emails.
risk: Escaped normal text renders as entity codes in plain-text email previews.

scenario: escapeHtml('') returns ''
whyItMatters: Empty fields (optional message) must not cause the escape function to throw.
risk: TypeError on empty string crashes PIN reset email for jurors with no custom message.

scenario: escapeHtml('<script>') returns '<script>'
whyItMatters: Script tags in juror names or messages must be neutralized before rendering in email.
risk: Unescaped < or > allows script tags to execute in HTML-rendered email clients.

story: escapeHtml neutralizes a realistic XSS payload
scenario: escapeHtml('') returns fully-escaped string
whyItMatters: Real-world XSS payloads combine multiple special chars; all must be escaped together.
risk: Partial escaping allows crafted names to inject executable code into admin email clients.

story: readBearerToken strips 'Bearer ' prefix from Authorization header
scenario: readBearerToken(req) with 'Bearer mytoken' header returns 'mytoken'
whyItMatters: Auth validation compares raw token; including 'Bearer ' prefix would always fail auth.
risk: All export audit requests fail auth, preventing any export from being logged.

scenario: readBearerToken with 'bearer mytoken' (lowercase) also returns 'mytoken'
whyItMatters: HTTP header values are case-insensitive; clients may send lowercase 'bearer'.
risk: Case-sensitive match fails for valid tokens from some HTTP client implementations.

story: readBearerToken returns empty string when Authorization header is absent
scenario: request with no Authorization header → readBearerToken returns ''
whyItMatters: Empty string triggers the 'Missing bearer token' 401 response correctly.
risk: Throwing or returning null causes a TypeError instead of a clean 401 rejection.

story: toNullableNumber converts numeric values to Number
scenario: toNullableNumber(42) returns 42; toNullableNumber('7') returns 7
whyItMatters: Export details include row/project/juror counts; they must be numeric in the audit log.
risk: String counts fail numeric aggregation queries on audit_logs.

story: toNullableNumber returns null for null and undefined
scenario: toNullableNumber(null) and toNullableNumber(undefined) both return null
whyItMatters: Optional count fields must be stored as SQL NULL, not 0 or 'null'.
risk: Storing 0 for missing counts skews export analytics aggregations.

story: toNullableNumber returns null for non-numeric strings
scenario: toNullableNumber('abc') returns null
whyItMatters: Invalid count values must not be stored as NaN in the audit log.
risk: NaN in numeric column causes INSERT to fail or stores incorrect data.

story: normalizeExportDetails maps camelCase aliases to snake_case output fields
scenario: rowCount, projectCount, jurorCount, periodName are mapped to snake_case keys
whyItMatters: Browser clients send camelCase; the audit log schema uses snake_case.
risk: Unmapped fields are silently dropped, leaving audit log with null counts.

scenario: normalizeExportDetails({format:'XLSX'}).format === 'xlsx'
whyItMatters: Audit queries filter by format; mixed case would split 'xlsx' and 'XLSX' into two groups.
risk: Format analytics show half the exports under 'XLSX' and half under 'xlsx', breaking reports.

story: writeAuditLog calls rpc_admin_write_audit_event with correct event shape
scenario: writeAuditLog invokes supabase.rpc with p_event containing action, org, user, resource fields
whyItMatters: writeAuditLog is the direct audit write path; its RPC call must be structurally correct.
risk: Wrong parameter name silently drops audit events with no error surfaced to caller.

story: writeAuditLog forwards the action string to the RPC unchanged
scenario: writeAuditLog('juror.created', {...}) calls rpc with p_event.action === 'juror.created'
whyItMatters: Action string is the primary audit log classifier; mutation would corrupt audit queries.
risk: Wrong action string causes audit events to appear under the wrong category.

story: writeAuditLog includes organization_id in the RPC call
scenario: writeAuditLog with organizationId option passes it as p_event.organization_id
whyItMatters: Org-scoped audit queries depend on organization_id; missing it makes the event invisible to tenant admins.
risk: Tenant admin audit log shows no events for their org despite activity.

story: writeAuditLog rejects when supabase RPC returns an error
scenario: when supabase.rpc resolves with {error: {message:'fail'}}, writeAuditLog throws
whyItMatters: Audit write failures must surface to callers so they can abort the guarded operation.
risk: Silent swallow allows the guarded action to proceed with no audit record.

story: writeAuthFailureEvent does not throw even when the RPC fails
scenario: when supabase.rpc rejects, writeAuthFailureEvent resolves without throwing
whyItMatters: Auth failure logging must not crash the login screen if the RPC is down.
risk: Uncaught error on auth failure logging crashes the login page, blocking all logins.

scenario: revokeEntryToken calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_revoke_entry_token', ...) — audit is DB-side
whyItMatters: Token revocation and its audit event must be atomic; non-atomic paths could skip the log.
risk: Token revoked without audit event → no forensic record of who revoked it and when.

story: revokeEntryToken forwards periodId to the RPC
scenario: revokeEntryToken('period-1') calls rpc with p_period_id === 'period-1'
whyItMatters: Wrong period ID would revoke the wrong token, denying legitimate jury access.
risk: Wrong period revocation locks out jurors for the wrong evaluation period.

story: revokeEntryToken throws when the RPC returns an error
scenario: when rpc_admin_revoke_entry_token returns {error:{message:'fail'}}, revokeEntryToken throws
whyItMatters: Revocation failures must surface so the UI can inform the admin instead of showing false success.
risk: Admin sees success toast but token is still active; jurors can continue submitting.

scenario: logExportInitiated invokes the log-export-event edge function with the correct action
whyItMatters: All exports must be logged server-side; skipping the edge function call creates an unlogged export.
risk: Export file generated with no server-side audit record — compliance gap.

story: logExportInitiated throws when action does not start with 'export.'
scenario: logExportInitiated({action:'download.csv'}) throws before calling edge function
whyItMatters: Only export.* actions are valid; non-export actions should use a different audit path.
risk: Wrong action prefix bypasses edge function validation, producing malformed audit events.

area: audit — logExportInitiated throws on edge function error
story: logExportInitiated throws when invokeEdgeFunction returns an error
scenario: edge function error causes logExportInitiated to throw, preventing the export
whyItMatters: Export must be aborted when audit logging fails — no file without a log.
risk: Silent swallow allows export file to be generated with no audit record.

scenario: forceCloseJurorEditMode calls supabase.rpc with the correct RPC name
whyItMatters: Force-close action is a privileged operation; its audit event must be atomic with the state change.
risk: State changed without audit event leaves no record of which admin forced edit-mode close.

story: listAuditLogs includes organization_id filter when provided
scenario: listAuditLogs({orgId:'org-1'}) applies .eq('organization_id','org-1') to the query
whyItMatters: Tenant admins must only see their own org's audit events — org filter enforces data isolation.
risk: Missing filter leaks other tenants' audit events to the requesting admin.

area: audit — listAuditLogs no org filter for super-admin
story: listAuditLogs returns all events when no orgId filter is passed
scenario: listAuditLogs({}) does NOT apply an organization_id eq filter
whyItMatters: Super-admins need cross-tenant visibility; applying an org filter would hide all events.
risk: Super-admin audit view is empty because the filter is accidentally applied with null.

area: ClearAllCriteriaModal — confirm button gated by text match
story: Delete All button disabled until confirmText matches displayName
scenario: confirm button is disabled when confirmText does not match displayName; enabled when it does
whyItMatters: Accidental destruction of all criteria is irreversible; text-match gate prevents mis-clicks.
risk: Admin accidentally deletes all criteria for a live evaluation period.

area: DeleteCriterionModal — confirm button gated by canDelete prop
story: Remove Criterion button disabled when canDelete is false
scenario: confirm button is disabled when canDelete=false; enabled when canDelete=true
whyItMatters: Criteria with associated scores cannot be safely deleted; canDelete gate enforces this.
risk: Admin deletes a scored criterion, corrupting evaluation results.

story: listAuditLogs includes action filter when provided
scenario: listAuditLogs({action:'export.scores'}) applies .eq('action','export.scores') to the query
whyItMatters: Action filter enables admins to search for specific event types in the audit log.
risk: Missing action filter returns all events when the admin is looking for a specific action.

area: audit — createJuror writes to jurors table
story: createJuror persists to the correct Supabase table
scenario: createJuror calls supabase.from('jurors') with the insert payload
whyItMatters: Juror records must land in the jurors table for audit trail resource IDs to resolve correctly.
risk: Wrong table or missing call means juror creation silently fails or corrupts another table.

area: audit — updateJuror writes to jurors table
story: updateJuror mutates the correct Supabase table
scenario: updateJuror calls supabase.from('jurors') with the update payload
whyItMatters: Juror updates must land in the jurors table for downstream audit events to reference valid data.
risk: Wrong table means juror profile changes are silently lost.

area: audit — deleteJuror writes to jurors table
story: deleteJuror deletes from the correct Supabase table
scenario: deleteJuror calls supabase.from('jurors') with a delete operation
whyItMatters: Juror deletions must target the jurors table to avoid orphaned records.
risk: Wrong table causes a juror to appear deleted in UI while the DB row remains.

scenario: resetJurorPin calls supabase.rpc('rpc_juror_reset_pin', ...)
whyItMatters: PIN reset must use the designated SECURITY DEFINER RPC so audit events are written atomically.
risk: Wrong RPC or direct table update bypasses the audit write.

scenario: unlockJurorPin calls supabase.rpc('rpc_juror_unlock_pin', ...)
whyItMatters: PIN unlock must use the designated RPC to ensure lock-state transitions are audited.
risk: Wrong RPC means unlock events are not recorded in the audit log.

area: audit — createProject writes to projects table
story: createProject persists to the correct Supabase table
scenario: createProject calls supabase.from('projects') with the insert payload
whyItMatters: Project records must land in the projects table for evaluation scoring to reference valid IDs.
risk: Wrong table means projects are invisible to jury evaluation and scoring.

area: audit — deleteProject writes to projects table
story: deleteProject deletes from the correct Supabase table
scenario: deleteProject calls supabase.from('projects') with a delete operation
whyItMatters: Project deletions must target the correct table to avoid ghost projects appearing in evaluation.
risk: Wrong table causes projects to remain in the DB after the UI reports deletion.

area: audit — createPeriod writes to periods table
story: createPeriod persists to the correct Supabase table
scenario: createPeriod calls supabase.from('periods') with the insert payload
whyItMatters: Evaluation periods must be written to the periods table for all downstream features to function.
risk: Wrong table means new periods are never created, blocking the entire evaluation lifecycle.

area: audit — deletePeriod writes to periods table
story: deletePeriod deletes from the correct Supabase table
scenario: deletePeriod calls supabase.from('periods') with a delete operation
whyItMatters: Period deletions must target the periods table to remove the record and cascade-delete children.
risk: Wrong table leaves the period in place while the UI shows it as deleted.

story: setEvalLock calls the correct audit-embedded RPC
scenario: setEvalLock calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_set_period_lock', ...)
whyItMatters: Lock/unlock transitions must use the SECURITY DEFINER RPC so the audit event is written atomically with the period update.
risk: Direct table update bypasses the audit write, leaving no record of who locked the period.

story: publishPeriod calls the correct audit-embedded RPC
scenario: publishPeriod calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_publish_period', ...)
whyItMatters: Period publication must go through the RPC to record the transition event in the audit log.
risk: Direct status update skips audit, making the publish event invisible to compliance review.

story: closePeriod calls the correct audit-embedded RPC
scenario: closePeriod calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_close_period', ...)
whyItMatters: Period closure is a terminal state transition; the audit RPC records it atomically.
risk: Wrong code path closes the period without an audit entry, losing traceability.

scenario: duplicatePeriod calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_duplicate_period', ...)
whyItMatters: Period duplication uses a SECURITY DEFINER RPC to copy criteria and audit the action atomically.
risk: Manual copy without the RPC skips the audit entry and may omit criteria rows.

story: savePeriodCriteria calls the correct audit-embedded RPC
scenario: savePeriodCriteria calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_save_period_criteria', ...)
whyItMatters: Criteria saves are transactional: delete + insert + outcome-map rebuild + audit write happen atomically in the RPC.
risk: Client-side write bypasses the audit entry and may leave orphaned outcome maps.

scenario: generateEntryToken calls supabase.rpc('rpc_admin_generate_entry_token', ...)
whyItMatters: Token generation must go through the SECURITY DEFINER RPC so the token is created and audited atomically.
risk: Direct table insert creates a token without an audit entry, bypassing traceability.

story: resetJurorPin forwards jurorId and periodId to the RPC
scenario: resetJurorPin({jurorId:'j-abc', periodId:'p-xyz'}) calls rpc with p_juror_id and p_period_id
whyItMatters: Wrong param names silently reset the wrong juror's PIN or fail the RPC entirely.
risk: PIN reset targets wrong juror due to swapped or renamed parameters.

story: unlockJurorPin forwards jurorId and periodId to the RPC
scenario: unlockJurorPin({jurorId:'j-abc', periodId:'p-xyz'}) calls rpc with p_juror_id and p_period_id
whyItMatters: Wrong param names silently unlock the wrong juror or fail the RPC entirely.
risk: PIN unlock targets wrong juror due to swapped or renamed parameters.

story: setEvalLock forwards the enabled boolean as p_locked
scenario: setEvalLock('per-1', false) calls rpc with p_locked=false and p_period_id='per-1'
whyItMatters: A swapped boolean would lock instead of unlock (or vice versa), corrupting period state.
risk: Period gets locked when admin intended to unlock, blocking jury access.

area: audit — publishPeriod passes correct period ID
story: publishPeriod forwards the periodId as p_period_id
scenario: publishPeriod('per-99') calls rpc with {p_period_id: 'per-99'}
whyItMatters: Publishing the wrong period could expose unready evaluation data to jurors.
risk: Wrong period ID publishes a different period, making it live before it is ready.

area: audit — closePeriod passes correct period ID
story: closePeriod forwards the periodId as p_period_id
scenario: closePeriod('per-99') calls rpc with {p_period_id: 'per-99'}
whyItMatters: Closing the wrong period would lock out jurors who are still evaluating.
risk: Wrong period ID closes an active period early, discarding in-progress evaluations.

area: audit — generateEntryToken passes correct period ID
story: generateEntryToken forwards the periodId as p_period_id
scenario: generateEntryToken('per-77') calls rpc with {p_period_id: 'per-77'}
whyItMatters: Generating a token for the wrong period would give jurors access to an unintended evaluation.
risk: Wrong period ID grants jury entry to a period the admin did not intend to open.

story: OPTIONS preflight returns 200 with CORS headers
scenario: OPTIONS request → 200 with access-control-allow-origin *
whyItMatters: Cron callers may probe with OPTIONS; missing CORS breaks scheduler integrations.
risk: Failed preflight blocks the hourly sweep when triggered via proxies.

scenario: POST with no secret header returns 401 "Unauthorized: invalid or missing cron secret".
whyItMatters: The function runs with service role; leaking it to unauthenticated callers would allow anomaly log pollution.
risk: Anyone on the internet could trigger sweeps or inject anomaly rows.

scenario: POST with mismatched secret header returns 401.
whyItMatters: Constant-equality check must reject forged secrets.
risk: Bypass allows unauthorized sweep execution.

scenario: GET → 405 Method not allowed.
whyItMatters: Handler is POST-only; stray GETs from misconfigured cron jobs must fail loudly.
risk: Silent no-op swallows misconfiguration.

scenario: Without SUPABASE_URL/SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, authenticated cron call returns 500.
whyItMatters: Service role client cannot be constructed without env; sweep must abort cleanly.
risk: Silent partial execution corrupts state.

story: Empty window + healthy chain → checked:true, anomalies:0, chain_ok:true
scenario: No audit rows in 60-min window, _audit_verify_chain_internal returns [].
whyItMatters: Baseline behavior under normal load must return a stable response shape.
risk: Silent regressions in anomaly counting or chain reporting.

story: audit_logs fetch error → 500 Failed to fetch audit logs
scenario: Mocked SELECT error propagates as 500 with details message.
whyItMatters: Cron must see a real error, not a silent 200 with empty sweep.
risk: Broken sweep looks healthy to observers.

story: Chain verify RPC failure → chain_ok=false, chain_error populated
scenario: _audit_verify_chain_internal errors → response body shows chain_ok:false + error string.
whyItMatters: Fail-closed invariant: never report chain_ok=true when verification itself failed.
risk: Silent false-positive chain health could mask real tampering.

story: Broken chain writes security.chain.broken row with severity=critical
scenario: verify returns broken link array → audit_logs insert with action=security.chain.broken, severity=critical, actor_type=system.
whyItMatters: A broken hash chain is an integrity event that must be recorded and routed through alerting.
risk: Tampering goes undetected.

story: Sweep-written rows have actor_type=system, user_id=null, no IP/UA
scenario: Inspecting inserted audit_logs payload confirms cron-written rows never carry user identity or request metadata.
whyItMatters: Anomaly rows are system-authored; attributing them to a user would falsify the audit trail.
risk: System events misattributed to users.

scenario: 200 body contains all documented fields.
whyItMatters: Downstream dashboards and alerts consume this shape; silent field removal breaks them.
risk: Monitoring breaks without a test canary.

scenario: OPTIONS → 200 + ACAO *.
whyItMatters: Jury Locked Recovery screen is cross-origin from the functions host.
risk: Locked jurors cannot request a reset from the browser.

scenario: Payload without periodId returns 400 Missing required fields.
whyItMatters: Function resolves org from periodId; no period = no recipient = email misfire.
risk: Silent no-op email or wrong-tenant leak.

scenario: Payload without jurorName returns 400.
whyItMatters: Admin needs the juror identity in the email subject to act.
risk: Ambiguous email ignored by admin.

scenario: resolvePeriodInfo returns null → 404 Period not found.
whyItMatters: Stale / forged period ids must not emit emails.
risk: Cross-tenant email if a later lookup silently defaulted.

story: No admin memberships and no org contact → 404
scenario: resolveAdminEmails returns [] and organizations.contact_email is empty → 404.
whyItMatters: Abort before writing audit log or sending half-configured emails.
risk: No recipient = silent dropped request.

story: Fallback to organizations.contact_email when no memberships → 200
scenario: No org_admin rows but contact_email set → 200 ok:true, sent:false (Resend unset).
whyItMatters: Small tenants without explicit org_admin seats still receive reset notifications.
risk: Locked jurors cannot escalate at tenants without admin seats configured.

story: Invalid JSON body → 500 with error message
scenario: Malformed JSON triggers catch block → 500 ok:false with parse error.
whyItMatters: Documents current (swallow) behavior; anchors future hardening.
risk: Behavior drift goes unnoticed.

story: Writes security.pin_reset.requested with severity=medium, actor_type=juror, resource=juror_period_auth
scenario: Success path inserts into audit_logs with the documented shape.
whyItMatters: PIN reset is a security event; audit trail is mandatory for compliance.
risk: Missing audit row = invisible lockout attacks.

risk: Missing CORS header blocks all admin entry token email delivery.

scenario: GET request → 405 {error: 'Method not allowed'}
whyItMatters: Only POST is valid; other methods must be rejected immediately.
risk: Unexpected methods may bypass validation or CORS handling.

story: Missing required fields returns 400 before auth
scenario: POST without recipientEmail or tokenUrl → 400 {error includes 'Missing required fields'}
whyItMatters: Field validation before auth prevents unnecessary DB roundtrips.
risk: Incomplete payload reaches auth layer and may cause unexpected DB errors.

scenario: Valid payload + no Authorization header → 401
whyItMatters: Unauthenticated callers cannot send entry token emails.
risk: Anonymous callers can send entry tokens to arbitrary emails.

scenario: authGetUser returns error → 401
whyItMatters: Expired or forged tokens must be rejected.
risk: Token replay allows attackers to send entry URLs to jurors.

scenario: Valid JWT + memberships returns null → 403
whyItMatters: Only org_admin or super_admin may send entry token emails.
risk: Regular users can distribute entry tokens they shouldn't have access to.

scenario: Admin auth passes + no RESEND key → 200 {ok:true, sent:false, error:'RESEND_API_KEY not configured'}
whyItMatters: Missing email config must not crash; caller gets a clear degraded signal.
risk: Hard failure breaks entry token distribution flow on unconfigured envs.

story: Resend success returns 200 sent:true and writes audit log
scenario: Admin auth + RESEND_API_KEY + Resend 200 → 200 {ok:true, sent:true} + audit_logs insert
whyItMatters: Happy path confirms full delivery pipeline and compliance audit record.
risk: Entry token email sent without audit record; compliance and traceability gap.

story: OPTIONS preflight returns 200 with CORS headers
scenario: OPTIONS request → 200 with access-control-allow-origin: *
whyItMatters: Browser CORS preflight must succeed for admin-triggered password change notifications.
risk: Missing CORS header blocks password change notification calls from browser.

scenario: GET request → 405 {error: 'Method not allowed'}
whyItMatters: Only POST is supported; other methods must be rejected.
risk: Unintended methods bypass the expected POST-only flow.

scenario: POST with no Authorization header → 401 {error: 'Missing bearer token'}
whyItMatters: Unauthenticated callers cannot trigger password change notifications.
risk: Anonymous callers flood users with fake password-changed emails.

story: Missing env vars returns 200 no-op immediately
scenario: Valid token + no RESEND_API_KEY → 200 {ok:true} without sending email
whyItMatters: Unconfigured email delivery should be a no-op not a crash; caller does not need to know.
risk: Hard failure on missing RESEND key breaks password change flow in non-email envs.

scenario: authGetUser returns error → 401 {error: 'Unauthorized'}
whyItMatters: Expired tokens must be rejected before any notification is sent.
risk: Token replay triggers password-changed emails for arbitrary users.

scenario: Valid auth + RESEND key + Resend returns 429 → 500 with error message
whyItMatters: Unlike soft-failure functions, password-changed-notify propagates Resend failure as 500.
risk: Silent swallow of Resend failure → admin thinks notification was sent when it was not.

scenario: Valid auth + RESEND_API_KEY + Resend 200 → 200 {ok:true}
whyItMatters: Happy path verifies full notification delivery including CC logic.
risk: Misconfiguration causes password change notification to never be sent.

story: OPTIONS preflight returns 200 with CORS headers
scenario: OPTIONS request → 200 with access-control-allow-origin: *
whyItMatters: Browser CORS preflight must succeed for unlock request notifications from admin UI.
risk: Missing CORS header blocks unlock request notification calls.

scenario: Malformed JSON body → 500 {ok:false, error contains message}
whyItMatters: Invalid JSON must not cause unhandled crash; outer try/catch must handle it.
risk: Unhandled parse error leaks stack trace or returns non-JSON response.

scenario: POST without type → 400 {ok:false, error includes 'Missing required fields'}
whyItMatters: type determines whether to notify super-admins or requester; missing = undefined behavior.
risk: Null type causes wrong recipients to be notified.

scenario: POST without request_id → 400 {ok:false, error includes 'Missing required fields'}
whyItMatters: request_id is required to identify which unlock request was submitted.
risk: Null request_id causes the audit log to record a null resource_id.

scenario: type=request_submitted + no RESEND key → 200 {ok:true, sent:false, error:'RESEND_API_KEY not configured'}
whyItMatters: Missing email config should degrade gracefully, not crash.
risk: Hard failure on missing RESEND key blocks unlock request submission.

scenario: type=request_submitted + no super_admin memberships → 200 {ok:true, sent:false, error includes 'No super-admin emails'}
whyItMatters: If no super-admins are configured, the function must degrade gracefully.
risk: Crash or empty To: field in Resend call causes 422 error.

story: request_submitted path with RESEND returns 200 sent:true
scenario: type=request_submitted + super-admin emails resolved + Resend 200 → 200 {ok:true, sent:true}
whyItMatters: Happy path verifies admin notification for new unlock requests.
risk: Super-admin never notified of unlock request; user stuck waiting.

story: request_resolved path with RESEND returns 200 sent:true
scenario: type=request_resolved + requester_user_id resolved + Resend 200 → 200 {ok:true, sent:true}
whyItMatters: Happy path verifies requester notification when their unlock is approved/denied.
risk: Requester never notified of resolution; submits duplicate requests.
